steps.dialogue: [event:6983d64ce7b9d3a5]We just learned that equivalent fractions like[event:6983d68617ade484] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=3]one third[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=2 denominator=6]two sixths[/fraction] land at the[event:6983d7c3eb2d1b0c] same position.

[event:697a7441bda79422]Now let me show you how we prove equivalent fractions using position with [event:697a835b74f8dd15][fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]two fourths[/fraction]...
First, I place[event:697a8046f92dce7e] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction].[event:697a8046f92dce7e] [event:697a8003d9282165]It goes right at the middle. Halfway between 0 and 1.

Next, I count to place [fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]two fourths[/fraction]:[event:697a880d65ac74c6] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=4]one fourth[/fraction], [fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]two fourths[/fraction]. [fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]Two fourths[/fraction][event:6983dc058e03eecd] lands at exactly the same position as [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction]!
[event:6983df17e2251074]Equivalent fractions can be proven two ways:[event:697b97bd009a2420] same amount shaded in bars, AND[event:697baad3ea1cde92] same position on the number line.
Are you ready to try finding equivalent fractions?[event:697bb9991784e4f0] I'll guide you through it. First, place [fraction numerator=2 denominator=3]two thirds[/fraction] on the top line.

prompt.text: Click to place [fraction numerator=2 denominator=3]two thirds[/fraction] on the top line.

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Let's take another look. Remember to count the thirds.

metadata.description: Vertical alignment guide appears between the two number lines

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]You're getting there. The key is:[event:697d1c5bada2a405] [fraction numerator=2 denominator=3]two thirds[/fraction] is two out of 3 intervals. Try counting the intervals.

metadata.description: Animate placement showing 2/3 on top line, then count sixths on bottom line showing 4/6 landing at same position

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Let's work together. For [fraction numerator=2 denominator=3]two thirds[/fraction]: [event:697d212c221d7996]count [fraction numerator=1 denominator=3]one third[/fraction], [fraction numerator=2 denominator=3]two thirds[/fraction].  [fraction numerator=2 denominator=3]Two thirds[/fraction] goes[event:697d2177da73d675] here. Two-thirds of the way from 0 to 1.

on_correct.dialogue: [event:697d1bcdb074e6f0]Good job! You placed [fraction numerator=2 denominator=3]two thirds[/fraction] on the top line correctly.

steps.dialogue: Now place [fraction numerator=4 denominator=6]four sixths[/fraction] on the bottom line.

prompt.text: Click to place [fraction numerator=4 denominator=6]four sixths[/fraction] on the bottom line.

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Let's take another look. [fraction numerator=2 denominator=3]Two thirds[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=4 denominator=6]four sixths[/fraction] are equivalent. Place them at the same point.

metadata.description: Vertical alignment guide appears

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]You're getting there. The key is: [fraction numerator=4 denominator=6]four sixths[/fraction] should[event:697d23761d9da1b4] line up directly below [fraction numerator=2 denominator=3]two thirds[/fraction].

metadata.description: Animate counting sixths and placing point at 4/6 position

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Let's work together. Count:[event:697d23cee1c4a581] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=6]one sixth[/fraction], [fraction numerator=2 denominator=6]two sixths[/fraction], [fraction numerator=3 denominator=6]three sixths[/fraction], [fraction numerator=4 denominator=6]four sixths[/fraction]! Same[event:697d2456fee0e628] position as [fraction numerator=2 denominator=3]two thirds[/fraction]!

on_correct.dialogue: Right. Thats where [event:69839de73df504a3] [fraction numerator=4 denominator=6]four sixths[/fraction] goes! The fractions land at the [event:697d22e64655e88b]same position. Another way to prove they're equivalent.

steps.dialogue: You followed our problem-solving method:[event:697d25edc6f1ffc3] equivalent fractions share the same position.

[event:697d2722735b6ca5]This time, try using our problem-solving method by yourself. Place [fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]two fourths[/fraction] on top and [fraction numerator=4 denominator=8]four eighths[/fraction] on bottom. Remember the steps: place each fraction, then check if they line up.

prompt.text: Drag a point to [fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]two fourths[/fraction] on the top line, then drag a point to [fraction numerator=4 denominator=8]four eighths[/fraction] on the bottom line.

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Let's look once more. [fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]Two fourths[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=4 denominator=8]four eighths[/fraction] both equal [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction]. Place them at the same point.

metadata.description: Vertical alignment guide appears between the two number lines

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]You're getting there. The key is:[event:697d29e76e0b4199] [fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]two fourths[/fraction] is halfway, and[event:697d2a57ab6d22f8] [fraction numerator=4 denominator=8]four eighths[/fraction] is four jumps out of eight, also halfway.

metadata.description: Animate placement with counting showing both fractions landing at the midpoint

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Let's work together.[event:6983b65bd7e194fd] [fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]Two fourths[/fraction] goes [event:697d2b1b0318b2f9]here: halfway. For [fraction numerator=4 denominator=8]four eighths[/fraction]: count [event:697d2bb3d19ee99a][fraction numerator=1 denominator=8]one eighth[/fraction], [fraction numerator=2 denominator=8]two eighths[/fraction], [fraction numerator=3 denominator=8]three eighths[/fraction], [fraction numerator=4 denominator=8]four eighths[/fraction].[event:697d2bd957fc202d] Same position! When dots line up vertically, fractions are equivalent. There we go.

on_correct.dialogue: Right! When the fractions [event:697d293713a3458e]line up, one directly above the other, they're[event:6983a3222ef62efe] [vocab]equivalent[/vocab]. That word equivalent means the fractions equal the same amount.

steps.dialogue: [event:697d2cc793c09038]Equivalent fractions are equal whether you use [event:6983b91a2ea1f545]bars or number lines.

[event:697d2e2395a4c16a]This bar shows [fraction numerator=4 denominator=4]four fourths[/fraction], four parts, and[event:6983bda46b5a3dec] all four parts[event:6983c26e6096b5c1] are shaded.[event:6983bfbb73d843a4] Where do you think [fraction numerator=4 denominator=4]four fourths[/fraction] will land on the number line?

prompt.text: Place a point on the number line where [fraction numerator=4 denominator=4]four fourths[/fraction] will land.

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Not quite. We're counting fourths, not getting to 4. [fraction numerator=4 denominator=4]Four fourths[/fraction] means all 4 parts. Where does that complete whole land?

metadata.description: All 4 bar sections pulse to highlight they represent one complete whole

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Remember:[event:697d2feacf9a9993] when we have all the parts: 4 out of 4, we have one[event:6983c921b6928ab6] complete whole. Place the point where one whole is on the numberline.

metadata.description: Animate counting through fourths to reach 1, then place point at 1

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]You can count with me, and we'll work this one out [event:697d31929a0079cd]together: [fraction numerator=1 denominator=4]one fourth[/fraction], [fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]two fourths[/fraction], [fraction numerator=3 denominator=4]three fourths[/fraction], [fraction numerator=4 denominator=4]four fourths[/fraction]. When we have all 4 parts out of 4, that's one complete whole, which equals 1.

on_correct.dialogue: Yes. [fraction numerator=4 denominator=4]Four fourths[/fraction] equals exactly 1![event:697d2feacf9a9993] When you have all the parts, you have [event:6983c921b6928ab6]one whole.

steps.dialogue: [event:6983ea154821d68d]When the [event:6983ea3eacb10e33]top number and bottom number match, the fraction equals[event:6983ea5988dab33e] 1. This rule is always true.

[event:697d33ffc2d0ad8e]These bars can introduce us to a new idea. Each bar shows [fraction numerator=4 denominator=4]four fourths[/fraction], one complete whole. Together[event:6983f1e69714ea34] they show [fraction numerator=8 denominator=4]eight fourths[/fraction].
If one bar equals 1, what do you think [fraction numerator=8 denominator=4]eight fourths[/fraction] equals?

prompt.text: Click the number you think 8/4 equals.

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Not quite. We're counting how many wholes, not how many fourths. Each bar is one whole. How many wholes do we have?

metadata.description: Each bar pulses in sequence to help student count

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Almost. [event:6983f2932149cfe1]One bar equals 1, but we have two[event:697d3742c2b6318d] bars here. Count them: how many complete wholes?

metadata.description: Highlight each bar showing it equals 1, then show both together equal 2

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Let me show you. Each bar shows [fraction numerator=4 denominator=4]four fourths[/fraction], which equals 1 whole.[event:697d3787b66abac1] We have two bars, so two complete wholes[event:697d3850bbd8d5d8] equals 2.

steps.dialogue: [fraction numerator=8 denominator=4]Eight fourths[/fraction] equals 2. Next, can you show me where [fraction numerator=8 denominator=4]eight fourths[/fraction] will land on the number line?

prompt.text: Place a point on the number line where [fraction numerator=8 denominator=4]eight fourths[/fraction] will land.

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Let's try this one again. Remember, [fraction numerator=8 denominator=4]eight fourths[/fraction] equals 2, so it should land at 2.

metadata.description: Number line shows grouping: 0 to 1, then 1 to 2 with visual separation

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Almost.[event:697d3a1906d31b7d] [fraction numerator=4 denominator=4]Four fourths[/fraction] equals 1. So if [fraction numerator=8 denominator=4]eight fourths[/fraction] is twice as many fourths, where does 2 go?

metadata.description: Animate counting beyond 1, highlighting each fourth as counted, landing at 2

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Let's work through this together. Count with me:[event:697d3b7f2694d2a7] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=4]one fourth[/fraction], [fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]two fourths[/fraction], [fraction numerator=3 denominator=4]three fourths[/fraction], [fraction numerator=4 denominator=4]four fourths[/fraction] equals 1. Keep going,[event:697d413f07729ddf] [fraction numerator=5 denominator=4]five fourths[/fraction], [fraction numerator=6 denominator=4]six fourths[/fraction], [fraction numerator=7 denominator=4]seven fourths[/fraction], [fraction numerator=8 denominator=4]eight fourths[/fraction] equals 2.[event:697d41d6a34384ea] [fraction numerator=8 denominator=4]Eight fourths[/fraction] is two complete groups of fourths, so it lands at 2.

on_correct.dialogue: Right! Let's count[event:697d449115489603] by fourths together. [fraction numerator=8 denominator=4]Eight fourths[/fraction] lands exactly at 2!

steps.dialogue: [fraction numerator=8 denominator=4]Eight fourths[/fraction] equals 2. [event:697d452865f7ab1d]When we have more fourths than it takes to make one whole, the fraction goes past 1.

We just saw that [fraction numerator=8 denominator=4]eight fourths[/fraction] equals 2. [event:69827cb1aeed1fcb] Now here's another way to write [event:69827ca42b9fceab] whole numbers as fractions. [d:.1] When the denominator is[event:697d49c1bf999a7f] 1, each piece is [event:69828145a7956d12] one whole.[d:.5][event:6982833767acba7c] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=1]One over one[/fraction] is [event:69828cd2754f3364] one whole-sized piece.
[event:6980bc4edf268f8c]When the denominator is 1, the fraction equals the numerator. So where will [fraction numerator=2 denominator=1]two over one[/fraction] land?

prompt.text: Place a point on the number line where [fraction numerator=2 denominator=1]two over one[/fraction] lands.

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]When the denominator is 1, the fraction equals the numerator. The numerator in [fraction numerator=2 denominator=1]two over one[/fraction] is 2, so it lands at...

metadata.description: Position 2 pulses gently on the number line

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start][event:6980bd7d492e5477][fraction numerator=1 denominator=1]One over one[/fraction] lands at 1. [fraction numerator=2 denominator=1]Two over one[/fraction] is one more whole. Where does that land?

metadata.description: Point appears at 2 on the number line

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Let me show you. When the denominator is 1, you're counting wholes.[event:6980be7777f09505] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=1]One over one[/fraction] is 1 whole, lands at 1. [event:6980beb4bc52c7e1][fraction numerator=2 denominator=1]Two over one[/fraction] is 2 wholes, lands at 2.

on_correct.dialogue: Right! [fraction numerator=2 denominator=1]Two over one[/fraction] equals 2.[event:6980bd07c85f7047] Two whole-sized[event:6983fb1aae0f0126] pieces land at 2.

steps.dialogue: When the denominator is 1, the fraction equals the numerator.[event:6980bfda9c573823] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=1]One over one[/fraction] equals 1. [event:6980c027809d8b75][fraction numerator=2 denominator=1]Two over one[/fraction] equals 2. It's another way to write whole numbers as fractions.

[event:6980c0b939c201dc]This pattern continues for all whole numbers. [fraction numerator=3 denominator=1]Three over one[/fraction] equals 3, [fraction numerator=4 denominator=1]four over one[/fraction] equals 4, and so on.

We've seen three fractions that all equal one: [fraction numerator=4 denominator=4]four fourths[/fraction] equals 1, [fraction numerator=6 denominator=6]six sixths[/fraction] equals 1, and [fraction numerator=8 denominator=4]eight fourths[/fraction] equals 2. What's the pattern? How can you tell if a fraction equals a whole number?

prompt.text: Click all the statements that are true. You can choose more than one.

options: When the top and bottom numbers are the same, the fraction equals 1
When the top number is exactly double the bottom number, the fraction equals 2
The bigger the bottom number, the bigger the fraction

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Try looking at the three fractions we found: [fraction numerator=4 denominator=4]four fourths[/fraction] equals 1, [fraction numerator=6 denominator=6]six sixths[/fraction] equals 1, and [fraction numerator=8 denominator=4]eight fourths[/fraction] equals 2. What do you notice about their top and bottom numbers?

metadata.description: The numbers in 4/4, 6/6, and 8/4 pulse to highlight the relationships

step.dialogue: [event:69810aeff8e9f812]Keep thinking it through. When top and bottom match, like[event:69810d02b8a24ff3] [fraction numerator=4 denominator=4]four fourths[/fraction] or [event:69810f1a5c6998fa][fraction numerator=6 denominator=6]six sixths[/fraction], you get 1. When the top is double the bottom,[event:698110ed1b7a1e0c] like [fraction numerator=8 denominator=4]eight fourths[/fraction], you get 2.

metadata.description: Show the two bars representing 4/4 each, pulse individually then together showing how 8/4 makes 2 wholes

step.dialogue: [event:69810aeff8e9f812]Let me show you the patterns. First pattern: when the top equals the bottom, like[event:69810d02b8a24ff3] [fraction numerator=4 denominator=4]four fourths[/fraction] or [event:69810f1a5c6998fa][fraction numerator=6 denominator=6]six sixths[/fraction], the fraction equals 1. That's one complete whole. Second pattern: when the top is double the bottom,[event:698110ed1b7a1e0c] like [fraction numerator=8 denominator=4]eight fourths[/fraction], the fraction equals 2. Look at 8 and 4: eight is exactly twice 4, so [fraction numerator=8 denominator=4]eight fourths[/fraction] gives us 2 complete wholes.[event:698117ab5816cbdf]

on_correct.dialogue: [event:69810aeff8e9f812]Right! When numerator and denominator match, like[event:69810d02b8a24ff3] [fraction numerator=4 denominator=4]four fourths[/fraction] or [event:69810f1a5c6998fa][fraction numerator=6 denominator=6]six sixths[/fraction], you get 1. And when the numerator is exactly double,[event:698110ed1b7a1e0c] like [fraction numerator=8 denominator=4]eight fourths[/fraction], you get 2!

steps.dialogue: [event:6980c559d9382507]Let's check that second pattern with [fraction numerator=8 denominator=4]eight fourths[/fraction]. We know[event:6980c58dd6e4cc2d] [fraction numerator=4 denominator=4]four fourths[/fraction] equals 1.[event:698404cb3afc4507] Eight is double 4, so[event:6980c5b5519dcf0c] [fraction numerator=8 denominator=4]eight fourths[/fraction] equals 2 complete wholes. See how that works?
Looking at the relationship between the numerator and denominator is how we identify whole numbers.
[event:6980c6340b85d765] let's use what you know about position in a new way. Look at this line.[event:6980c64dabc41998] It shows [fraction numerator=3 denominator=2]three halves[/fraction].

[event:698405ef518fba44]This bottom line has a fraction at the same position as [fraction numerator=3 denominator=2]three halves[/fraction]. That means they're [event:6984072534798f88]equivalent. Place the correct fraction below the point on the bottom number line.

prompt.text: Place the fraction that is equivalent to [fraction numerator=3 denominator=2]three halves[/fraction] on the bottom number line.

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Not quite. Think about where [fraction numerator=3 denominator=2]three halves[/fraction] lands: past 1 but before 2. Which fraction from the bank would land at the exact same spot?

metadata.description: The mark on the bottom line pulses at the position matching 3/2

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]You're working on it. Here's what helps:[event:6980cbb1155c023f] [fraction numerator=3 denominator=2]three halves[/fraction] is three halves. Think about fourths: [event:6980cc0c63835a34][fraction numerator=4 denominator=4]four fourths[/fraction] equals 1, then [fraction numerator=5 denominator=4]five fourths[/fraction], [fraction numerator=6 denominator=4]six fourths[/fraction]. Which one matches three halves?

metadata.description: Animate counting showing 6/4 landing at same position as 3/2

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Let me help you think through this. [fraction numerator=3 denominator=2]Three halves[/fraction] means:[event:6980cf464bdbd355] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction], [fraction numerator=2 denominator=2]two halves[/fraction] (that's 1), then [fraction numerator=3 denominator=2]three halves[/fraction], lands here[event:698408e0a253d65a] between 1 and 2. Now count fourths:[event:6980cf8c04c0b745] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=4]one fourth[/fraction], [fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]two fourths[/fraction], [fraction numerator=3 denominator=4]three fourths[/fraction], [fraction numerator=4 denominator=4]four fourths[/fraction] (that's 1), [fraction numerator=5 denominator=4]five fourths[/fraction], [fraction numerator=6 denominator=4]six fourths[/fraction], lands at the[event:698408a7e4918774] same spot. So [fraction numerator=6 denominator=4]six fourths[/fraction] is the answer.

on_correct.dialogue: Right! You recognized that [fraction numerator=6 denominator=4]six fourths[/fraction][event:6980c939a1a9e44e] lands at the same position as [fraction numerator=3 denominator=2]three halves[/fraction][event:6984069187e56f60]. They're [event:698406bbac51cf64]equivalent fractions: different names for the same position!

steps.dialogue: Now let's practice without the number line showing positions. You know equivalent fractions share the same position. Click all of the fractions that are equivalent to [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction].

prompt.text: Click all the fractions that are equivalent to [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction]. You can choose more than one.

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Think about [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction]. It lands halfway to 1. Which of these would also land halfway?

metadata.description: Highlight the relationship between numerator and denominator in each correct fraction

step.dialogue: [event:6980e2291f56477a]Here's what helps:[event:6980e38ba79fbf61] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction] is half of the whole.[event:6980e3cd79cc89ef] [fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]Two fourths[/fraction] is 2 out of 4, also half.[event:6980e550c7a99e6b] [fraction numerator=3 denominator=6]Three sixths[/fraction] is 3 out of 6, also half. Think about which numerators are half of their denominators.

metadata.description: Correct fractions pulse to show they are all equivalent to 1/2

step.dialogue: [event:6980e6ff9a44becf]Let me help you reason through this.[event:6980e7155924aec6] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]One half[/fraction] means half. [event:6980e737e149b346][fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]Two fourths[/fraction]: is 2 half of 4? Yes, same position.[event:6980e75a1e75f8b0] [fraction numerator=3 denominator=6]Three sixths[/fraction]: is 3 half of 6? Yes, same position.[event:6980e78e8eabcf93] [fraction numerator=4 denominator=8]Four eighths[/fraction]: is 4 half of 8? Yes, same position. [event:6980ec64a0961f4f]The others don't have that relationship.

on_correct.dialogue: Right! You found all three without even seeing a number line.

steps.dialogue: Let's review what you've practiced today. You learned two important ideas [event:69828dfde0ce0962]. [d:.5] First: you can identify equivalent fractions by [event:6980ff0c6a304ded] checking if they land at the same position on a number line. [event:6980ff064545978a][fraction numerator=1 denominator=3]One third[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=2 denominator=6]two sixths[/fraction] land at the same spot: equivalent.[event:69810048cfe30bb6] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]One half[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]two fourths[/fraction] land at the same spot: equivalent. Position proves they're equal.[event:69840d4aff0408ad]

Second: you can recognize fractions that equal whole numbers. When the numerator and denominator match, like [event:69810438b05892b9][fraction numerator=4 denominator=4]four fourths[/fraction] or [fraction numerator=6 denominator=6]six sixths[/fraction], the fraction equals 1. When the numerator is bigger, like [event:698104d0245b0605][fraction numerator=8 denominator=4]eight fourths[/fraction] or [fraction numerator=6 denominator=3]six thirds[/fraction], it can equal 2 or more.
You've discovered that different fractions can share the same position, and that some fractions represent complete wholes. We will use these ideas as we continue to work with fractions.
